name: Context Management & Reliability
tagline: Keep long agents coherent, honest, and cheap — and handle every way a request can end.
overview: The smallest domain, but the one that separates a demo from a production agent. It tests whether you can budget the context window, keep a long conversation alive with compaction, context editing, and memory used correctly, and make a system reliable: provenance, escalation, honest status, caching, refusal handling, retries, and every stop reason.
objectives: Budget the context window: count tokens with the count_tokens endpoint (never tiktoken), leave headroom, and keep max_tokens distinct from the window.
Distinguish compaction (summarises near the limit), context editing (prunes stale results), and memory (persists across sessions) — and append the compaction blocks back.
Build reliability in: information provenance, escalation gates, honest status reporting, and defence against error propagation.
Handle a safety refusal correctly — a successful response with stop_reason 'refusal' — by checking stop_reason before reading content.
Lean on the SDK's automatic 429/5xx backoff and respect retry-after instead of hand-rolling retries, and branch on every stop reason.

lessons.title: Token budgeting and the context window
lessons.objective: Measure tokens accurately, leave headroom, and never confuse the window with the output cap.
lessons.body:
## Two different limits

Every request lives inside two separate budgets, and conflating them is the classic mistake:

- **The context window** — the total the model can see: `tools` + `system` + every message + the space reserved for the reply. On current models this is large (1M tokens), but it is finite and shared by the whole conversation.
- **`max_tokens`** — the cap on the *output* only. It is **not** the context window; it carves out room *within* it for the response. Set it too low and the reply is truncated mid-thought (`stop_reason: "max_tokens"`); set it absurdly high and you crowd out input.

## Count tokens the right way

To measure a prompt, call the **`count_tokens` endpoint** (`POST /v1/messages/count_tokens`) with the *same model id* you'll run — token counts are model-specific.

```python
resp = client.messages.count_tokens(
    model='claude-opus-4-8',
    messages=[{'role': 'user', 'content': open('brief.md').read()}],
)
print(resp.input_tokens)
```

**Do not use `tiktoken`.** It is OpenAI's tokenizer; it undercounts Claude by ~15-20% on prose and far more on code or non-English text. Any budget built on a `tiktoken` estimate is wrong, and the error is always in the dangerous direction — you think you have headroom you don't.

## Leave headroom

Never fill the window to the brim. Reserve space for the output (`max_tokens`), for tool results you can't predict the size of, and for the model's own thinking. When input plus reserved output approaches the limit you hit `stop_reason: "model_context_window_exceeded"` — a *different* signal from `max_tokens`, and one you handle by compacting or splitting, not by raising the output cap.

## Why the exam cares

The budgeting question is almost always a trap with `tiktoken` or with `max_tokens` mislabelled as 'the context window'. Know that counting is an API call against the real tokenizer, and that the output cap and the window are two different numbers.

knowledgeCheck.stem: You need to know whether a 40-page document will fit in the context window before sending it. What's the correct way to count its tokens?

options.text: Call the count_tokens endpoint with the same model id you'll use for inference
Run it through tiktoken and add the message overhead
Divide the character count by four
Set max_tokens to the window size and let the API tell you

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Token counts are model-specific, so you call `count_tokens` with the model you'll actually run. `tiktoken` is OpenAI's tokenizer and undercounts Claude; character/4 is a rough heuristic, not a budget; and `max_tokens` is the output cap, not the window.

knowledgeCheck.stem: A request returns `stop_reason: "max_tokens"`. What does that tell you?

options.text: The output hit the max_tokens cap and was cut off — raise it or stream
The whole conversation exceeded the context window
The model refused the request for safety reasons
A server-side tool loop paused at its iteration cap

knowledgeCheck.explanation: `max_tokens` caps the *output*; hitting it truncates the reply, and the fix is a higher cap or streaming. Exceeding the whole window is the separate `model_context_window_exceeded` reason; the others are `refusal` and `pause_turn`.

lessons.title: Compaction, context editing, and memory
lessons.objective: Use the three context tools for what each is for — and append the compaction blocks back.
lessons.body:
## Three tools, three jobs

Long agents drown in their own history. There are three mechanisms, and the exam's favourite question is whether you know which does what:

| Mechanism | What it does | Scope |
|---|---|---|
| **Compaction** | **Summarises** earlier context server-side when you near the window | Within a session |
| **Context editing** | **Prunes** stale tool results / old thinking blocks past a threshold | Within a session |
| **Memory** | **Persists** facts the agent reads and writes | **Across** sessions |

Compaction *summarises*; editing *removes*; memory *persists*. Many long-running agents use all three at once.

## Compaction: append the blocks back (the #1 gotcha)

Compaction is beta (`compact-2026-01-12`). When the conversation approaches the window, the API summarises earlier turns server-side and returns a **compaction block** in `response.content`. The API uses that block to replace the compacted history on your *next* request.

The critical rule: **append `response.content` back — not just the text.**

```python
resp = client.beta.messages.create(
    betas=['compact-2026-01-12'],
    model='claude-opus-4-8', max_tokens=16000, messages=messages,
    context_management={'edits': [{'type': 'compact_20260112'}]})
# Append the FULL content — the compaction block must survive
messages.append({'role': 'assistant', 'content': resp.content})
```

If you extract only `resp.content[0].text` and append *that*, you silently throw away the compaction state. The next request re-sends the full uncompacted history, the summary is lost, and you're back to blowing the window — with no error to tell you why.

## Context editing: prune, don't summarise

Context editing clears old tool results and completed thinking blocks once they cross a configurable threshold. It keeps the transcript lean **without** summarising — the cleared content is gone, not condensed. Reach for it when old tool outputs are no longer relevant but the conversation structure should stay intact.

## Memory: the only cross-session tool

Compaction and editing both operate *within one session*. To remember a fact in next week's session, the agent needs a **memory store / memory tool** it reads and writes — survives process restarts. If a question says 'across separate sessions', the answer is memory, never compaction.

## The summarisation risk

Summarisation is lossy by design. Each compaction pass can drop a detail a later turn turns out to need; over a very long run this is **context degradation**, and models are position-sensitive about what they retain. Don't treat compaction as free — for high-stakes facts, also write them to memory or pin them so a summary can't quietly erase them.

knowledgeCheck.stem: Your long chat enables compaction, but after a while the agent keeps losing the thread and the window keeps filling up. You append `resp.content[0].text` to the history each turn. What's wrong?

options.text: You must append the full response.content (including the compaction block), not just the text — appending only the text silently discards the compaction state
Compaction only works if you also enable context editing in the same request
You forgot to lower max_tokens, so there's no room to compact
Compaction needs a memory store attached to persist the summary

knowledgeCheck.explanation: The API returns a compaction block in `response.content` and uses it to replace the compacted history next turn. Appending only the extracted text drops that block, so every request re-sends the full uncompacted history and the summary is lost — with no error.

knowledgeCheck.stem: An agent must recall a customer's preferences in a brand-new session next week. Which mechanism fits?

options.text: A memory store / memory tool the agent reads and writes
Compaction
Context editing
A larger max_tokens

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Compaction and context editing both operate only within a single session. Cross-session persistence is exactly what memory is for — it survives process restarts.

knowledgeCheck.stem: Which statement correctly contrasts context editing with compaction?

options.text: Editing removes stale tool results and old thinking blocks; compaction summarises earlier context into a block
Editing summarises old turns; compaction deletes them
Both summarise, but editing runs across sessions
Both persist state to disk between runs

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Context editing prunes (removes) stale content past a threshold; compaction summarises near the limit and returns a compaction block. Neither persists across sessions — that's memory.

lessons.title: Reliability: refusals, retries, provenance, and honest status
lessons.objective: Handle every stop reason, lean on SDK backoff, and make the agent's output verifiable and honest.
lessons.body:
## Check stop_reason before reading content

A safety refusal is **not an error** — it's a successful `HTTP 200` with `stop_reason: "refusal"`. The trap: a *pre-output* refusal can return an **empty `content` array**, so code that reads `response.content[0]` unconditionally throws on exactly those responses.

```python
resp = client.messages.create(model='claude-opus-4-8', max_tokens=1024, messages=messages)
if resp.stop_reason == 'refusal':
    handle_refusal()        # content may be empty (pre-output) or partial (mid-stream)
else:
    print(resp.content[0].text)
```

Always branch on `stop_reason` first. The full set you must handle: `end_turn`, `tool_use`, `pause_turn`, `max_tokens`, `refusal`, and `model_context_window_exceeded`.

## Don't hand-roll retries

The SDK **automatically retries** `429` and `5xx` with exponential backoff — default ~2 retries, configurable via `max_retries`. It also reads and respects the `retry-after` header. Writing your own retry loop usually just fights the built-in one and ignores `retry-after`. Only hand-roll when you need genuinely custom behaviour (e.g. a circuit breaker), and even then, configure `max_retries` rather than disabling it blindly.

## Caching for cost and reliability

Prompt caching is a **prefix match**: any byte change anywhere in the prefix invalidates everything after it. Render order is `tools` -> `system` -> `messages`. So keep the **stable prefix first** (frozen system prompt, deterministic tool list) and put **volatile content last** (timestamps, per-request ids, the varying question). A `datetime.now()` in the system prompt silently busts the cache on every call — verify with `usage.cache_read_input_tokens`.

## Provenance: track where a fact came from

In a multi-step agent, a fact three tools deep is worthless if you can't say *where it came from*. **Information provenance** means carrying the source alongside each fact so a human (or the agent) can verify it — which document, which tool result, which URL. It's the antidote to confident fabrication and the basis of honest status reporting.

## Error propagation and escalation

A bad tool result early in a run **poisons** every later step that builds on it — error propagation. Defend against it with validation at tool boundaries, and with **escalation / human-review gates** for irreversible or low-confidence actions: when the agent isn't sure, it should stop and ask, not guess.

## Honest status reporting

The agent must report what actually happened — 'tests failed', 'I skipped step 3', 'this is unverified' — not a cheerful summary that papers over a gap. Ground every progress claim against a real tool result. An agent that lies about success is worse than one that fails loudly.

knowledgeCheck.stem: Your code does `text = response.content[0].text` and occasionally throws an index error. The status code is always 200. What's the likely cause?

options.text: A safety refusal returned stop_reason 'refusal' with an empty content array — check stop_reason before indexing content
The request was rate-limited and returned no body
max_tokens was zero, so content is always empty
The model id was wrong, returning a 200 with an empty payload

knowledgeCheck.explanation: A refusal is a successful 200 with `stop_reason: "refusal"`; a pre-output refusal has an empty `content` array, so unconditional `content[0]` access throws. Branch on `stop_reason` first.

knowledgeCheck.stem: You're seeing intermittent 429s under load. What's the recommended first move?

options.text: Rely on the SDK's automatic 429/5xx exponential backoff (respecting retry-after); tune max_retries if needed
Write a custom retry loop with a fixed 1-second sleep
Ignore the retry-after header and retry immediately
Switch off retries and surface every 429 to the user

knowledgeCheck.explanation: The SDK already retries 429 and 5xx with exponential backoff (default ~2 retries) and respects `retry-after`. Hand-rolling usually fights that and ignores the header; tune `max_retries` rather than reinventing it.

questions.stem: An agent has compaction enabled but keeps re-exceeding the context window. Logs show the full early history is re-sent on every request. The dev appends `''.join(b.text for b in resp.content if b.type == 'text')` each turn. What's the fix?

options.text: Append the whole resp.content (compaction block included) back to messages, not a reconstructed text string
Add a memory store so the compaction summary persists
Lower max_tokens so compaction has room to run
Switch from compaction to context editing — compaction doesn't summarise

questions.explanation: The compaction block lives in `response.content`; the API needs it to replace the compacted history next turn. Rebuilding a text-only string drops the block, so the uncompacted history is re-sent every request — silently, with no error.

questions.stem: Match each mechanism to what it actually does.

options.text: Compaction summarises, context editing prunes, memory persists across sessions
Compaction prunes, context editing summarises, memory caches
All three summarise; only the trigger threshold differs
Compaction and editing both persist across sessions; memory is per-request

questions.explanation: Compaction condenses earlier context into a block, context editing removes stale tool results / old thinking, and memory is the only one that survives across separate sessions. Caching is a separate cost/latency optimisation.

questions.stem: Why is `tiktoken` the wrong tool for sizing a Claude prompt?

options.text: It's OpenAI's tokenizer and undercounts Claude tokens — use the count_tokens endpoint with the target model
It's accurate but too slow for production
It only counts output tokens, not input
It requires an API key Claude users don't have

questions.explanation: `tiktoken` is built for OpenAI models and undercounts Claude (~15-20% on prose, more on code/non-English). Accurate counts come from `count_tokens` against the same model you'll run.

questions.stem: A response arrives with HTTP 200 and `stop_reason: "refusal"`. How should the application treat it?

options.text: As a successful, intentional outcome — surface the refusal; don't blindly retry the same prompt, and don't index content before checking stop_reason
As a transient server error to retry with backoff
As a malformed request to fix and resend unchanged
As proof the API key lacks permission for the model

questions.explanation: A refusal is a deliberate, successful response (`stop_reason: "refusal"`), not an error. Pre-output refusals can have empty content, so check `stop_reason` first; retrying the identical prompt will just refuse again.

questions.stem: Which prompt-caching layout maximises cache hits?

options.text: Stable prefix first (frozen system prompt, deterministic tool list); volatile content (timestamps, varying question) last
Volatile content first so it's processed before the cache breakpoint
Interleave a fresh timestamp into the system prompt each request
Reorder the tool list per request to keep it fresh

questions.explanation: Caching is a prefix match — any byte change in the prefix invalidates everything after it. Render order is tools -> system -> messages, so keep stable content first and push volatile content (timestamps, ids, the question) after the breakpoint. A `datetime.now()` in the system prompt silently busts every cache.

questions.stem: In a multi-step research agent, what does tracking information provenance buy you?

options.text: The ability to verify each fact against the source it came from (document, tool result, URL)
A larger effective context window
Automatic deduplication of tool calls
Faster token counting

questions.explanation: Provenance carries the source alongside each fact so a human or the agent can check it. It's the defence against confident fabrication and the foundation of honest status reporting.

questions.stem: A 6-hour agent's final summary claims 'all migrations applied and tests green', but the run log shows two tool calls returned errors it never surfaced. Which reliability principle was violated?

options.text: Honest status reporting — progress claims must be grounded against actual tool results, not a cheerful summary
Token budgeting — the summary exceeded max_tokens
Prefix caching — the summary invalidated the cache
Compaction — the errors were summarised away

questions.explanation: An agent that reports success it can't evidence is failing on honest status reporting. Each claim should be audited against a real tool result; failures and skipped steps must be stated plainly.

questions.stem: A tool returns malformed data early in an agentic run, and every later step builds on it and goes wrong. What is this failure mode, and the standard defence?

options.text: Error propagation — validate at tool boundaries and add escalation/human-review gates for low-confidence or irreversible actions
Context degradation — fix it by lowering effort
Cache invalidation — fix it by freezing the system prompt
A refusal — fix it by retrying on another model

questions.explanation: A bad early tool result poisoning downstream steps is error propagation. You contain it by validating tool outputs at the boundary and gating risky or uncertain actions behind escalation rather than letting the agent build on bad data.

questions.stem: Which stop reasons must a robust agentic loop branch on explicitly? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: refusal
max_tokens
model_context_window_exceeded
pause_turn

questions.explanation: All four need distinct handling: `refusal` is a successful safety decline (check before reading content); `max_tokens` truncates output (raise it or stream); `model_context_window_exceeded` means compact or split; `pause_turn` means a server-tool loop paused — resume by re-sending. (The non-terminal happy paths `end_turn` and `tool_use` round out the set.)

questions.stem: Which are accurate statements about the three context-management mechanisms? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: A long-running agent can use compaction, context editing, and memory together
Each progressive-summarisation pass can drop a detail a later turn needs
Memory is the only one of the three that persists across separate sessions
Context editing summarises old turns into a compact block

questions.explanation: Compaction, editing, and memory are complementary and often used together; summarisation is lossy, so each pass risks dropping a needed detail (context degradation); and memory alone spans sessions. The false option describes compaction — context editing *prunes*, it doesn't summarise.

scenarios.title: Keep a 6-hour agent coherent and honest
scenarios.tagline: Window pressure, the compaction gotcha, persistence, and truthful reporting.
scenarios.context: You run an unattended 6-hour research-and-build agent. The conversation grows to hundreds of turns and approaches the context window. Findings discovered early must still be usable late, results must be verifiable, and the final report must be trustworthy. You're the architect.

steps.prompt: The conversation is approaching the context window. What's the first lever?

choices.text: Enable compaction so earlier context is summarised server-side as you near the limit
choices.rationale: Compaction is purpose-built for nearing the window: it condenses earlier history into a block so the run can continue. It's the right primary tool for window pressure inside a session.

choices.text: Truncate the oldest messages client-side and hope nothing important was dropped
choices.rationale: Blind truncation silently deletes context the agent may still need. Compaction summarises rather than guessing what to discard.

choices.text: Add context editing to prune stale tool results, alongside compaction
choices.rationale: Editing genuinely helps by removing dead tool outputs, and the two compose well — but on its own it doesn't address the summarise-near-the-limit need that compaction covers.

steps.prompt: You enable compaction. How do you handle the response each turn?

choices.text: Append the full response.content (including the compaction block) back to messages
choices.rationale: The API returns a compaction block in content and uses it to replace the compacted history next turn. Appending the full content preserves that state — the single most important compaction rule.

choices.text: Extract and append only the assistant's text so the history stays small
choices.rationale: Appending only the text drops the compaction block; the next request re-sends the full uncompacted history and the summary is lost, silently, with no error.

choices.text: Append the full content, and also write key findings to a memory store
choices.rationale: Appending the full content is exactly right; adding memory is a sensible belt-and-braces for high-stakes facts that a summary might erode — slightly more than the step strictly asked, but not wrong.

steps.prompt: Early findings risk being summarised away, and the final report must be verifiable and honest. What do you build in?

choices.text: Track provenance per fact, pin/persist critical findings to memory, and ground every status claim against a tool result
choices.rationale: Provenance makes facts checkable, persisting critical findings protects them from lossy summarisation, and grounding claims against real results gives honest status reporting — directly addressing degradation, verifiability, and trust.

choices.text: Trust compaction to retain what matters and let the agent summarise its own success at the end
choices.rationale: Summarisation is lossy and self-reported success without evidence is exactly the dishonest-status failure to avoid. Neither verifiability nor durability is addressed.

choices.text: Persist findings to memory and add citations, but skip auditing the final status against tool results
choices.rationale: Persistence and provenance cover durability and verifiability, but skipping the status audit leaves the door open to a cheerful-but-false summary — the honesty gap remains.

scenarios.title: Stabilise a flaky high-volume endpoint under rate limits
scenarios.tagline: Retries, refusals, and caching — three calls under real load.
scenarios.context: A high-traffic endpoint wraps a single Claude call. Under load it throws intermittent 429s, occasionally crashes on certain inputs, and your token costs are climbing. You're the architect tasked with stabilising it.

steps.prompt: The 429s are spiking. What's the right first response?

choices.text: Rely on the SDK's built-in exponential backoff for 429/5xx and respect retry-after; tune max_retries
choices.rationale: The SDK already retries 429/5xx with backoff and honours retry-after. Leaning on it (and tuning max_retries) is correct, less code, and avoids fighting the built-in behaviour.

choices.text: Add a tight custom loop that retries immediately on any 429, ignoring retry-after
choices.rationale: Immediate retries that ignore retry-after hammer the API and worsen the rate-limit situation, while duplicating logic the SDK already provides.

choices.text: Disable SDK retries and surface every 429 to the caller to handle upstream
choices.rationale: Pushing retries upstream can be a deliberate architecture choice for a queue, but as a default it just exposes transient blips the SDK would have absorbed for free.

steps.prompt: The endpoint occasionally crashes with an index error reading the response, always on a 200. What's the cause and fix?

choices.text: Some inputs trigger a refusal (stop_reason 'refusal') with empty content; check stop_reason before reading content[0]
choices.rationale: A pre-output refusal is a 200 with an empty content array; unconditional content[0] access throws on exactly those. Branching on stop_reason first is the fix.

choices.text: Wrap the content access in a try/except and return a generic error on failure
choices.rationale: It stops the crash, but masks a legitimate refusal as a generic error and loses the chance to handle it properly — a band-aid over the real cause.

choices.text: Retry the request on the same model until content is non-empty
choices.rationale: Retrying an identical prompt that was refused just refuses again, burning quota and latency for nothing.

steps.prompt: Token costs are climbing because every request re-sends a large fixed instruction block. What do you do?

choices.text: Add prompt caching with the stable instruction block in the prefix and the per-request input after the breakpoint
choices.rationale: A large fixed prefix is the ideal cache target. Keep it first (stable), put the varying input last, and cache reads cost a fraction of fresh input — verified via cache_read_input_tokens.

choices.text: Cache the prompt but interpolate the current timestamp into the system block for logging
choices.rationale: Any byte change in the prefix invalidates the cache; a per-request timestamp in the system block silently busts it every call, so you pay the write premium and never get a read.

choices.text: Move the instruction block to a memory store the model reads each call
choices.rationale: Externalising shared context is reasonable, but it doesn't directly cut per-request input tokens the way a stable cached prefix does — caching is the more targeted fix here.

labs.title: Run a compacting conversation without losing state
labs.goal: Prove to yourself that the compaction block must be appended back — by breaking it, then fixing it.
labs.steps:
## What you'll build

A multi-turn loop with compaction enabled, instrumented to show the difference between appending the full content and appending only the text.

## Steps

1. Start a `client.beta.messages.create` loop with `betas=['compact-2026-01-12']` and `context_management={'edits': [{'type': 'compact_20260112'}]}`.
2. Drive a long conversation (feed in a large document, then keep asking follow-ups) until compaction triggers.
3. **Broken path:** each turn, append only `resp.content[0].text` to `messages`. Watch `usage` and the resent history grow — the window keeps filling.
4. **Fixed path:** append the full `resp.content` instead. Confirm the compaction block survives and the resent history stays bounded.
5. Log `usage.input_tokens` + `cache_*` each turn so the difference is visible, not assumed.

## Why it matters

This is the single highest-value gotcha in the domain — and the one the exam tests hardest. Breaking it deliberately makes the rule stick.
checklist: Compaction beta header and context_management config are set
Broken path (text-only append) reproduces the runaway window
Fixed path (full content append) keeps the resent history bounded
usage is logged each turn so the difference is observed, not assumed

labs.title: Make a single call robust: stop reasons, retries, and budgeting
labs.goal: Wrap one Claude call so it handles every stop reason, leans on SDK backoff, and budgets tokens honestly.
labs.steps:
## Steps

1. Before sending, call `count_tokens` with the target model and assert the input leaves headroom for `max_tokens` — refuse to send (and log) if it doesn't. Do **not** use tiktoken.
2. Make the call and branch on `stop_reason`: handle `end_turn`, `tool_use`, `pause_turn` (resume by re-sending), `max_tokens` (raise/stream), `refusal` (surface, don't blind-retry), and `model_context_window_exceeded` (compact/split).
3. Check `stop_reason` **before** touching `response.content[0]` so a pre-output refusal can't throw.
4. Force a refusal with a clearly disallowed prompt and confirm your branch fires with an empty content array.
5. Leave SDK retries on; set `max_retries` explicitly and confirm a simulated 429 is retried with backoff (don't hand-roll the loop).

## Checkpoint

If your code reads `content[0]` before checking `stop_reason`, or contains a hand-written 429 retry loop, stop — both are the bugs this lab exists to remove.
checklist: count_tokens (not tiktoken) gates the send and reserves output headroom
Every stop reason has an explicit branch
stop_reason is checked before reading content[0]; a forced refusal is handled cleanly
SDK backoff is relied on with max_retries set; no hand-rolled 429 loop

resources.title: Compaction
resources.annotation: Setup, the trigger threshold, and the critical rule: append response.content (with the compaction block) back every turn.

resources.title: Context editing
resources.annotation: Thresholds and what gets cleared. It prunes stale tool results / thinking — it does not summarise.

resources.title: Context windows
resources.annotation: Window sizes and token management — and why max_tokens (output cap) is separate from the window.

resources.title: Token counting
resources.annotation: Use the count_tokens endpoint with the target model. Never tiktoken — it undercounts Claude.

resources.title: Errors & rate limits
resources.annotation: Error codes, the retryable set, SDK auto-backoff for 429/5xx, and respecting retry-after.

videos.title: D5 · Token budgeting & the context window
